primary_diagnosis.name: Vitiligo (mild non-segmental) with secondary acute health anxiety

pathophysiology_summary: Non-segmental vitiligo is an acquired autoimmune depigmenting disorder in which cytotoxic (CD8+) T-cell mediated destruction of epidermal melanocytes produces well-demarcated, completely depigmented (chalk-white) macules, characteristically symmetrical and favouring the dorsal hands, face and periorificial skin. The skin architecture is otherwise normal — no scale, no atrophy, preserved sensation — which distinguishes it from infective, post-inflammatory and neurocutaneous mimics. It is benign, non-contagious and of cosmetic (and psychosocial) significance only; the depigmented skin lacks melanin photoprotection and sunburns readily. In this case the dominant acute problem is not the skin lesion but the secondary panic: catastrophic misattribution to a contagious 'flesh-eating' process has driven a panic attack and obsessive washing with hot water and harsh soaps, producing a reactive irritant dermatitis of the surrounding skin. The telehealth task is to recognise the benign pattern, actively de-escalate the anxiety, halt the skin-damaging behaviour, and arrange calm confirmatory review — without either dismissing the patient or amplifying the panic into an inappropriate emergency presentation.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetrical, sharply demarcated chalk-white (fully depigmented) macules on the dorsal hands with no scale, itch or pain — the classic pattern.
The patient's own fear; must be actively excluded — but tinea causes a scaly, often itchy, active advancing/annular border, not uniform depigmentation.
Can cause pale patches, but they are hypopigmented with fine scale and favour the trunk, not stark depigmentation of the dorsal hands.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At intake the depigmented-symmetrical pattern points strongly to vitiligo; the value is in screening OUT the infective mimics rather than acting on the patient's contagion fear.

differential.evidence_basis: No scale/itch/pain, smooth intact surface, symmetrical dorsal-hand distribution, no systemic illness.
Excluded on history: complete absence of scale, active border, central clearing or pruritus. True depigmentation is not a feature of tinea.
Considered, but the lesions are stark chalk-white (fully depigmented) rather than merely lighter, with no preceding rash, burn or trauma at those sites.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Focused history (no scale, no itch, smooth surface, no preceding dermatosis) collapses the infective/post-inflammatory differentials toward benign vitiligo.

differential.diagnosis: Non-segmental vitiligo (presumptive) with secondary acute health anxiety and reactive irritant dermatitis from over-washing
differential.evidence_basis: Benign, non-contagious depigmentation; the acute, treatable problems are the panic and the self-inflicted irritant dermatitis. Definitive dermatological confirmation (and formal exclusion of leprosy via sensation testing) is deferred to in-person review.

A named mimic to consciously exclude; against it are fully preserved sensation within the patches (as reported) and absence of thickened peripheral nerves — but sensory testing and nerve palpation are clinician manoeuvres, so exclusion is only presumptive via telehealth.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct end-state is a confident presumptive diagnosis of benign vitiligo with reassurance and de-escalation, an explicit note that leprosy/derm confirmation needs an in-person exam, and NO emergency escalation.

red_flags.description: Signs of secondary bacterial skin infection / cellulitis — spreading redness, warmth, swelling, pain out of proportion, or fever (would follow the skin-barrier damage from over-washing)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The one genuinely time-sensitive complication in reach here: irritant-damaged skin can become secondarily infected. Currently absent, but the patient must be told exactly what would change the picture.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An evolving cellulitis dismissed as 'just the vitiligo/scrubbing' could progress to a systemic infection requiring urgent antibiotics.

red_flags.description: Severe acute anxiety / panic with compulsive skin-damaging washing driven by catastrophic contagion beliefs
red_flags.clinical_significance: The dominant acute morbidity. Untreated, the obsessive washing damages the skin barrier and the panic can drive an inappropriate emergency presentation; it needs active de-escalation and mental-health safety-netting.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Escalating panic, worsening irritant dermatitis, and a distressed, unnecessary late-night ED visit for a benign cosmetic finding.

red_flags.description: Sensory loss within the white patches or thickened peripheral nerves (leprosy red flag)
red_flags.clinical_significance: If a hypopigmented patch is anaesthetic or an adjacent nerve is thickened, the diagnosis is leprosy, not vitiligo — a notifiable, treatable infection. Reported sensation is normal, but definitive testing is an in-person manoeuvre.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed leprosy patch delays curative multidrug therapy and risks nerve damage.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being pulled by the patient's 'fungal infection / parasite eating my skin' framing into diagnosing and treating an infection that is not there (e.g. prescribing an antifungal), rather than reading the depigmented, non-scaly pattern for what it is.
Mistaking the intensity of the patient's panic for clinical acuity and escalating a benign cosmetic finding to the emergency department — the classic over-triage trap this case exists to test.
Reassuring about the skin but ignoring the acute health anxiety and obsessive washing, which are the actual treatable problems tonight.
Declaring vitiligo confirmed over telehealth without flagging that sensation testing and nerve palpation (leprosy exclusion) require an in-person exam.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Definitive dermatological assessment of lesion border, depigmentation depth and surface (Wood's lamp accentuation)
Formal sensation testing within the patches to exclude leprosy
Clinician-measured vital signs (BP, HR, temperature, respiratory rate, SpO2)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: No emergency escalation. Presumptive reassurance now, active anxiety de-escalation, stop the harsh washing, and arrange a routine in-person GP or dermatology review (within 2–3 weeks) to confirm the diagnosis, formally exclude leprosy by sensation testing, and discuss natural history and topical options.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the benign nature of the finding; the real risks are (a) over-reacting to the patient's panic with an ED referral, and (b) closing on 'vitiligo, confirmed' without noting that leprosy exclusion needs an in-person exam.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Vitiligo is benign and non-contagious; management includes photoprotection of depigmented skin, topical corticosteroids or calcineurin inhibitors for repigmentation, and psychological support; confirm clinically ± Wood's lamp.
A hypopigmented or reddish skin patch with definite loss of sensation is a cardinal sign of leprosy — sensation testing is required to distinguish it from other hypo/depigmenting conditions.
